content.text:
{A false balance is an abomination to the Lord, but a just weight is his delight.}{Proverbs 11:1}
 {Pure mathematics is, in its way, the poetry of logical ideas.}{— Albert Einstein}

The determinant satisfies several elegant properties that make it 
both theoretically beautiful and practically useful.

content.title: Effect of Row Operations on Determinants
content.content:
Let $A$ be a square matrix:

1. If $B$ is obtained by **interchanging two rows** of $A$, 
 then $\det(B) = -\det(A)$.

2. If $B$ is obtained by **multiplying a row by** $k$, 
 then $\det(B) = k \cdot \det(A)$.

3. If $B$ is obtained by **adding a multiple of one row 
 to another**, then $\det(B) = \det(A)$.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
These properties provide a faster method for computing determinants: 
row reduce to triangular form, track the operations, and multiply the 
diagonal entries.

content.title: Computing a determinant using row reduction
Determinant and Invertibility
content.content: A square matrix $A$ is invertible if and only if $\det(A) \neq 0$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Row reduce $A$ to echelon form $U$. The matrix $A$ is invertible iff 
$U$ has no zero rows, iff $U$ is triangular with all nonzero diagonal 
entries, iff $\det(U) \neq 0$. Since row operations multiply 
determinants by nonzero constants (or change sign), 
$\det(A) \neq 0 \Leftrightarrow \det(U) \neq 0$.

content.title: Multiplicative Property
content.content:
For square matrices $A$ and $B$ of the same size:
\[
\det(AB) = \det(A) \cdot \det(B)
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
This remarkable theorem says that determinants turn matrix 
multiplication into ordinary multiplication.
This means that all theorems about rows also apply to columns!

content.title: Consequences of Row Properties
content.content:
For an $n \times n$ matrix $A$:

1. If $A$ has two identical rows, then $\det(A) = 0$.

2. If $A$ has a row of zeros, then $\det(A) = 0$.

3. $\det(kA) = k^n \det(A)$ for scalar $k$.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Common Error
content.content:
Note that $\det(kA) = k^n \det(A)$, **not** $k\det(A)$. Every 
row gets multiplied by $k$.

marginNotes.text:
For with the judgment you pronounce 
you will be judged, and with the measure you use it will be measured 
to you.

marginNotes.author: Leopold Kronecker (1823--1891)
marginNotes.text:
God made the integers; 
all else is the work of man.
